model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 8","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 32","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current network is extremely small for a 256-step CIFAR-10 budget. A modest width increase should improve feature quality quickly without adding architectural complexity or changing training dynamics.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","old":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The widened model still has only one residual block. Adding a second same-width residual block is a small, coherent capacity increase that should improve representation quality within the existing optimizer and schedule.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last two gains came from adding capacity without destabilizing optimization. A further moderate width increase is the simplest next step and should improve feature quality more reliably than changing the optimizer or schedule midstream.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 32","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Capacity increases have been consistently effective so far. A further moderate width increase, paired with a larger classifier hidden layer, is the most direct way to keep improving representation quality without changing the training recipe.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","old":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Capacity increases have consistently reduced validation loss, and the current 32-channel model still has only two residual blocks. Adding one more same-width residual block is the smallest next architectural extension with a good chance of improving representation quality without changing optimization behavior.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The model has grown substantially and improvements from more depth are already tapering while runtime keeps rising. Label smoothing is a very low-complexity regularization change that often improves CIFAR-10 validation loss without affecting the training budget.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Label smoothing moved validation loss in the wrong direction on this workload. Reverting that regularization keeps the stronger architecture while restoring the better-performing loss setup.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The architecture has become substantially larger, while optimization has remained on plain Adam. Switching to AdamW is a low-complexity optimizer-family change that often improves validation loss on CNNs with weight decay, especially once model capacity is no longer the main bottleneck.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"AdamW slightly worsened validation loss on this fixed-budget run. Reverting to Adam restores the better optimizer while keeping the stronger architecture and schedule unchanged.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"With the larger network now stable under warmup-plus-cosine scheduling, a modest learning-rate increase is the simplest way to test whether optimization is still under-stepping within the fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The higher learning rate degraded validation loss, so the larger model appears close to the right step size already. Reverting to the previous rate restores the best-performing optimization scale before trying other directions.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed 256-step budget, increasing batch size lets the model see more unique training examples while keeping the optimizer, architecture, and schedule intact. This is a simple way to test whether the current setup is data-limited rather than capacity-limited.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The jump to batch size 128 produced the largest recent gain, which suggests this fixed-step workload is strongly data-limited. Pushing batch size further is the simplest continuation of that signal and could let the model cover roughly a full pass over the training set within 256 updates.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Increasing batch size has produced the strongest gains so far, indicating this fixed-step regime is dominated by how many training examples are covered per update budget. Moving from 256 to 512 is the most direct follow-up to test whether seeing roughly two full passes worth of examples within 256 steps helps further.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The training regime is now very different after scaling batch size to 512, and the previous learning-rate sweep was done at much smaller batches. A moderate LR increase is the simplest way to test whether the larger-batch setup is now under-updating within the same 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Both major recent gains came from increasing batch size, which indicates the fixed 256-step regime is still dominated by sample coverage rather than optimizer instability. Increasing to 1024 is the clearest next test of that trend while keeping the strong larger-batch learning rate unchanged.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The jump to 1024 broke verification, while 512 with the same optimizer and schedule was the current best configuration. Reverting to the last stable batch size restores the strongest known regime before using the remaining steps on safer refinements.","secondary_modes":[]}
